planting.note: Drill or broadcast 4-6 weeks before hard freeze so plants tiller before dormancy.

directions: Sow at 60-120 lb per acre or about 2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, drilling 1-1.5 inches deep or broadcasting onto a firm, moist seedbed. Winter wheat overwinters as a rosette, resumes growth in spring, and scavenges residual soil nitrogen while suppressing weeds. It is easier to terminate than cereal rye, making it a good choice for gardeners. Terminate in spring by mowing at the boot to early-flowering stage, then incorporate with tillage or crimp and leave residue as mulch. Allow two to three weeks of decomposition before planting the following crop.

planting.note: Sow in early spring as soon as soil can be worked for a fast summer smother crop.

directions: Broadcast or drill at roughly 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm seedbed and rake to cover about 1 inch deep. Spring oats germinate quickly in cool soil and produce dense biomass that outcompetes weeds and captures nitrogen. Unlike winter oats, spring oats will not survive hard freezes, which makes winterkill a convenient no-till termination in cold climates. In mild areas terminate by mowing before seed heads form, then incorporate or leave residue as surface mulch. Follow with a nitrogen-fixing legume to balance the carbon added by the grass residue.

planting.note: Plant in early to mid fall so plants establish crowns before winter dormancy.

directions: Drill or broadcast at 2-3 lb per 1,000 sq ft about 1 inch deep into a firm, well-drained seedbed. Winter barley tolerates drought and saline soils better than other cereals and releases allelopathic compounds that suppress weeds. It is less cold hardy than rye or wheat, so it performs best in USDA zones 6 and warmer. Terminate in spring by mowing or crimping at the boot stage before pollen shed, then incorporate or leave as mulch. Barley residue breaks down faster than rye, allowing earlier planting of the succeeding crop.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last frost when soil reaches at least 60 F.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a moist seedbed and rake lightly to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep. Japanese millet germinates fast in warm soil and tolerates temporarily wet or flooded ground better than most summer cover crops. It produces abundant biomass in as little as six to eight weeks and can be used to smother summer weeds in fallow beds. Terminate by mowing before seed set to avoid volunteers, then incorporate residue or leave as mulch. Frost will kill the crop, providing free termination ahead of fall or spring plantings.

planting.note: Wait until soil is thoroughly warm before sowing this heat-loving annual.

directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover about 0.5 inch deep in warm, firm soil. Foxtail millet is exceptionally drought tolerant and matures quickly, making it ideal for filling short summer windows between cash crops. Its fibrous roots improve soil tilth while the dense canopy shades out weeds. Mow before the seed heads harden to prevent reseeding, then incorporate or leave the residue as surface mulch. In cold regions frost will terminate any remaining plants at season's end.

planting.note: Sow in late summer or early fall for maximum biofumigant biomass before frost.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake lightly to cover 0.25 inch deep in a moist seedbed. Brown mustard is a high-glucosinolate brassica valued as a biofumigant that suppresses soilborne pests and weeds. For best biofumigation, mow or flail at early flowering when biomass peaks and immediately incorporate the chopped tissue into moist soil, then cover or roll to trap released compounds. Wait two to three weeks before planting sensitive crops to allow volatile compounds to dissipate. In cold winters the plants will winterkill, easing spring termination.

planting.note: Plant in late summer for fast fall biomass; it winterkills in most climates.

directions: Broadcast at roughly 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm, moist seedbed and rake to a shallow 0.25 inch depth. White mustard germinates rapidly in cool soil and is often grown to suppress cyst nematodes and weeds. It grows quickly but is less winter hardy than brown mustard, so it typically winterkills once temperatures drop into the low twenties. Terminate by mowing at early bloom and incorporating the residue, or simply let frost kill it and leave the residue as protective mulch. Follow with a spring vegetable once residue has broken down.

planting.note: Sow six to eight weeks before frost so plants form a strong rosette to overwinter.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25-0.5 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Winter-type oilseed rape develops a deep taproot that breaks up compacted subsoil and scavenges nitrogen and sulfur. Overwintering plants bolt and flower in spring, providing early forage for pollinators if allowed to bloom. Terminate by mowing at early flowering before pods set, then incorporate the residue with tillage. Allow two to three weeks of breakdown before planting the following crop to avoid nitrogen tie-up.

planting.note: Sow in late spring or summer; it is more heat tolerant than other mustards.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover roughly 0.25 inch deep in warm soil. Ethiopian mustard is more heat and drought tolerant than white or brown mustard, making it useful for summer biofumigation windows. Its high glucosinolate content suppresses nematodes and soilborne pathogens when the biomass is chopped and incorporated. Mow or flail at early flowering and immediately work the residue into moist soil, then irrigate or roll to seal the surface. Wait two to three weeks before planting a sensitive crop into the treated bed.

planting.note: Sow in late summer so plants bulk up before cold weather slows growth.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.2-0.4 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25 inch deep in a firm, fertile seedbed. Forage kale produces large amounts of leafy biomass and a fibrous root system that scavenges nitrogen and improves soil structure. It is very cold hardy and stays green well into winter, providing living cover and weed suppression during the cool season. Terminate by mowing or grazing before it bolts in spring, then incorporate the residue with light tillage. In colder zones repeated hard freezes may winterkill the crop, easing termination.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early fall for quick fall cover that scavenges nutrients.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.25-0.5 lb per 1,000 sq ft and rake to cover 0.25 inch deep in a moist seedbed. This fast-growing brassica establishes quickly, forming a fibrous root mat that takes up excess nitrogen and reduces leaching over winter. It is less cold hardy than oilseed rape and often winterkills in colder regions, providing free termination. Where it survives, mow at early flowering and incorporate the residue two to three weeks before planting. The rapid canopy closure also smothers fall-germinating weeds.

planting.note: Sow in early fall, often mixed with a cereal, so vines establish before frost.

directions: Inoculate seed with pea-vetch rhizobia and drill at 1.5-2 inches deep or broadcast at about 2-4 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm seedbed. Austrian winter pea is a viny legume that fixes substantial nitrogen and is frequently sown with cereal rye or oats for support and biomass. It overwinters in mild climates and resumes vigorous vining growth in spring. Terminate by mowing or crimping at early flowering when nitrogen fixation peaks, then incorporate or leave as mulch. The residue releases nitrogen quickly, benefiting a following heavy-feeding vegetable crop.

planting.note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions; it is less cold hardy than hairy vetch.

directions: Inoculate with vetch-pea rhizobia and broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, raking to cover 1 inch deep. Purple vetch is a fast-growing annual legume best suited to mild-winter climates where it fixes nitrogen through the cool season. It climbs readily, so pairing it with a cereal companion improves standability and biomass. Terminate by mowing or crimping at early bloom before viable seed forms to prevent weedy volunteers. Incorporate the succulent residue or leave it as a nitrogen-rich mulch two to three weeks before planting.

planting.note: Sow in early fall; it matures earlier than common hairy vetch.

directions: Inoculate seed and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering to 1 inch deep in a firm seedbed. Woolly pod vetch is an early-maturing relative of hairy vetch that fixes nitrogen and produces dense sprawling growth. Its earlier flowering makes it easier to terminate ahead of spring planting than standard hairy vetch. Mow or roller-crimp at early bloom for effective no-till kill, then plant into the residue mat. Because it can reseed, terminate before pods harden to limit volunteers in future crops.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or fall in mild climates; cold hardy to the low twenties.

directions: Inoculate with faba-bean rhizobia and sow large seed 2-3 inches deep at about 4-6 inches apart in rows or broadcast heavily. Faba beans are upright legumes that fix large amounts of nitrogen and improve soil structure with their sturdy taproots. They tolerate cool weather and wet soils, making them a strong early-spring green manure in temperate gardens. Terminate by mowing or cutting at early flowering when nitrogen fixation is greatest, then incorporate the succulent stems into the soil. Allow two to three weeks for decomposition before planting a following crop.

planting.note: Sow in spring; slow to establish but forms a long-lived rhizomatous stand.

directions: Inoculate with the specific kura clover rhizobia strain and broadcast at about 0.2-0.4 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a firm, weed-free seedbed no more than 0.25 inch deep. Kura clover establishes slowly during its first year because it invests heavily in rhizomes, but once established it forms a persistent living mulch. Its spreading roots fix nitrogen and provide erosion control and pollinator forage for many years. Manage rather than fully terminate it by mowing or suppressing with strip tillage so it regrows between cash crops. For complete removal, repeated tillage or cultivation is required because of its vigorous rhizomes.

planting.note: Sow in fall in mild-winter regions for late-season biomass and nitrogen.

directions: Inoculate with clover rhizobia and broadcast at about 0.2-0.3 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm seedbed, covering no deeper than 0.25 inch. Arrowleaf clover is a reseeding annual legume of the southern United States that produces high biomass and fixes nitrogen well into late spring. It is later maturing than crimson clover, extending the window of living cover and pollinator bloom. Terminate by mowing at early to mid bloom, then incorporate or leave residue as mulch. Allowing a portion to set seed lets the stand reseed naturally for the following season.

planting.note: Sow in spring into well-drained, calcareous soil; not tolerant of wet ground.

directions: Inoculate with the specific sainfoin rhizobia and drill or broadcast at about 1-2 lb per 1,000 sq ft, covering 0.5-1 inch deep in well-drained soil. Sainfoin is a deep-rooted perennial legume that fixes nitrogen, thrives on dry alkaline soils, and produces showy pink blooms that are excellent for pollinators. Its non-bloating forage and deep taproot make it a durable soil-building crop where drainage is good. Manage by mowing after the first flush of bloom to encourage regrowth, or terminate mature stands with tillage before planting a following crop. It performs poorly on acidic or waterlogged sites, so match it to suitable ground.

planting.note: Sow in early spring; the deep taproot exploits subsoil moisture and breaks compaction.

directions: Sow seed 1-1.5 inches deep at about 0.5-1 lb per 1,000 sq ft into warm, well-drained soil after the ground can be worked. Safflower develops a strong taproot that penetrates several feet, breaking up compacted subsoil and drawing up moisture and nutrients from depth. It is highly drought tolerant, making it a useful soil-building cover for dry sites and rotations. Terminate by mowing before the spiny flower heads mature, since the bracts become sharp and seed can volunteer. Incorporate the residue or leave it as surface mulch to protect the soil and improve organic matter.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; the deep taproot loosens compacted soil.

directions: Broadcast at about 0.1-0.2 lb per 1,000 sq ft onto a firm, weed-free seedbed and rake to a shallow 0.25 inch depth. Forage chicory is a deep-rooted perennial that mines nutrients from the subsoil, breaks up compaction, and provides mineral-rich forage and pollinator bloom when allowed to flower. It establishes as a rosette the first year and bolts in following seasons. Manage as a living cover by mowing to prevent flowering, or terminate by tillage or cutting the crown below the soil surface. Because it is persistent, mow or till before seed set to keep it from spreading beyond the intended area.
